title: What to Do If Your Cat Ate a Plastic Wrapper: Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Cat swallowed plastic? Learn if it's an emergency, what signs to watch for, and how a photo of the wrapper helps your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat eats a plastic wrapper, it is a medium-priority situation that requires veterinary consultation to prevent potential gastrointestinal blockages. Contact your vet and provide a photo of the material to determine if immediate intervention is necessary.

What happens if my cat eats a plastic wrapper?

When a cat ingests a plastic wrapper—whether it is from food, candy, or shipping materials—it is medically categorized as a foreign body ingestion. Because plastic is not digestible, it must either pass through your cat's entire digestive tract or be removed if it becomes lodged. Common materials include cellophane, crinkly food bags, and soft plastic films.

Is it an emergency if my cat swallowed a plastic wrapper?

  • The urgency level for this situation is Medium.
  • While small pieces of soft plastic may pass without assistance, there is always a risk of gastrointestinal obstruction or internal injury.
  • Contact an emergency vet immediately if your cat is currently choking, struggling to breathe, or showing signs of extreme distress.
  • If they are acting normally, you should still contact your veterinarian to discuss the size and type of plastic consumed.

How does a photo of the material help the veterinary triage process?

  • Taking a clear photo of the remaining packaging or an identical wrapper allows the medical team to assess the size, shape, and sharpness of the ingested material.
  • This information helps them determine if the item is likely to pass on its own or if it poses a high risk of getting stuck.
  • The visual assessment dictates whether the medical team needs to induce vomiting or perform imaging.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Foreign bodies, such as plastic, are usually objects that cannot be digested or are too large to pass through the GI tract, leading to obstruction. Cats more typically ingest linear foreign bodies like string, yarn, or dental floss. Obstruction secondary to foreign body ingestion can be partial or complete. Vomiting, depression, abdominal discomfort, and/or fever warrant reevaluation. Careful examination of the oral cavity in cats, looking for evidence of yarn, thread, or needles, is important. Abdominal radiographs should be performed, looking for radiopaque foreign objects or signs of intestinal distention, indicating possible obstruction. Small objects identified radiographically may pass through the GI tract and can be monitored with serial radiographs if the animal is clinically stable; failure to pass within 48 hours warrants further intervention.

Chapter: Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 392)

faqs.question: What symptoms should I look for if my cat ate plastic?
faqs.answer: Monitor your cat for vomiting, loss of appetite, lethargy, abdominal pain, or changes in their litter box habits, such as straining to defecate.

faqs.question: Should I try to induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting in a cat at home unless specifically directed by a veterinarian. Doing so incorrectly can cause aspiration pneumonia or damage to the esophagus.

faqs.question: How long does it take for plastic to pass through a cat?
faqs.answer: If it is going to pass, it typically takes between 10 and 24 hours. However, if the plastic becomes stuck in the stomach or intestines, it may remain there for days or weeks while causing progressive illness.
